summary: DHH uses the release of Top Gun: Maverick as a lens to discuss American cultural malaise and the value of optimistic storytelling. He contrasts current pessimism about America's direction with the reality that 1986—when the original Top Gun premiered—was objectively worse by many metrics, including triple the homicide rate in New York City. He argues that nostalgia makes the past seem brighter, but the real difference may be one of attitude and faith. The post concludes that America needs confident, unironic cultural touchstones like Top Gun to remind people that decline isn't destiny and turnaround is possible.
key_points: Top Gun: Maverick succeeds because it offers confident, unironic American optimism at a time of widespread cultural fatalism
America in 1986 was objectively worse on many metrics (e.g., 3x the homicides in NYC) despite being remembered as a happier era
The gap between nostalgia and reality suggests attitude and faith matter as much as material conditions
Cultural works that wallow in brokenness reinforce despair, while aspirational films can shift collective mindset
Historical comparison proves America can turn things around, but only if people believe it's possible
money_quotes: It's as subversive as an unironic American flag pin at a hipster café.
America was very cool to a Danish kid growing up in Copenhagen in the 80s.
There were over three times as many homicides in 1986 as there are today in New York City.
This moment right here, is just a snapshot. America isn't destined to head off the cliff.
But to turn things around, you need to have some faith that it can be done. Top Gun oozes that faith.
key_insight: Cultural pessimism blinds Americans to historical progress, and regaining faith in the possibility of turnaround—as embodied by Top Gun's unironic confidence—is a prerequisite for actually achieving it.
